title: India Pivots to Prabowo: Securing Defence and Digital Infrastructure in Indonesia's Transition
lede: Modi's pre-inauguration engagement with Indonesia's president-elect signals New Delhi's determination to lock in strategic gains before Prabowo's October takeover reshapes Jakarta's foreign policy architecture.

sections.context:
Prime Minister Modi's bilateral with Prabowo Subianto on the G20 sidelines in Brazil represents a calculated diplomatic move to establish India's baseline expectations before Indonesia's leadership transition. This is not routine statecraft. India is meeting Prabowo before his inauguration, not after — a sequencing choice that reflects both urgency and leverage. The window between September and October 2024 is strategically compressed: Prabowo's cabinet appointments, foreign policy recalibrations, and priority setting happen in those weeks. By securing commitments on the India-Indonesia Comprehensive Strategic Partnership now, Modi ensures these agreements carry implicit continuity obligations for the incoming administration.

The specific focus on defence cooperation and digital public infrastructure reveals India's prioritization calculus. BrahMos system exports represent a tier-one strategic asset — India's most advanced anti-ship cruise missile platform. That India brought this explicitly to the table, rather than letting it surface in working-group discussions, signals confidence that Prabowo's government will prove a receptive customer. This is notably different from India's approach to [[india-indonesia-defence-2020|previous defence negotiations with Jokowi's administration]], where platform diversification remained cautious. The DPI (Digital Public Infrastructure) export agenda connects to India's broader [[india-global-south-dpi-2023|push to position UPI and digital payment architecture as development toolkit for the Global South]]. Indonesia's 270 million population and digital adoption trajectory make it the primary testing ground for this export model.

Timing matters because Indonesia's strategic orientation under Prabowo remains partially undefined. His nationalist credentials, his past tensions with Western institutions, and his relationship with China create ambiguity that Delhi needs to resolve quickly through concrete commitments. By positioning itself as a ready partner on both military and digital infrastructure, India is essentially offering Prabowo an alternative to either Chinese dependency or benign neglect from the Western camp.
sections.threads:
This conversation sits within [[india-asean-strategic-autonomy-2022|India's sustained effort to deepen ASEAN partnerships as counterweight to Chinese regional dominance]], but with a critical difference in execution. Rather than speaking to ASEAN collectively through formal mechanisms, India is now moving bilaterally with individual leaders at inflection points. This reflects a maturation of India's ASEAN strategy: less focus on broad declarations, more focus on granular state-to-state architecture before regional alignments crystallize. Prabowo's presidency represents such an inflection point. His past statements on nationalist themes and his distance from Jokowi's consensus-driven diplomacy suggest Indonesia may recalibrate its South China Sea posture and defence partnerships.

The BrahMos dimension connects directly to [[india-southeast-asia-military-sales-2021|India's emerging role as a credible defence technology provider in Southeast Asia]]. India has successfully positioned itself as an alternative to Western systems (which carry political conditionality) and Chinese platforms (which raise sovereignty concerns). For Indonesia — an archipelago with complex maritime boundaries and Chinese presence in the South China Sea — an advanced cruise missile system offers both deterrence and signaling value. Yet this sale is not inevitable. It requires Prabowo's explicit buy-in, which is precisely what Modi sought to secure.

The digital infrastructure angle reveals India's understanding that 21st-century strategic partnership is no longer purely military. Indonesia's fintech ecosystem is competitive and domestically sophisticated, but UPI-style unified payment architecture could accelerate financial inclusion in rural regions — a priority for Prabowo's developmentalist positioning. By framing DPI exports as development partnerships rather than technology exports, India recasts the relationship from buyer-seller to co-builders of institutional capacity. This parallels [[india-africa-dpi-partnerships-2023|India's approach in Africa]], where DPI is presented as sovereignty-enhancing technology rather than dependency-creating.
sections.signal:
Watch for three specific developments in the next 12-18 months. First, monitor whether Indonesia issues formal RFQ (Request for Quotation) or LOI (Letter of Intent) for BrahMos systems by Q2 2025. This would signal that Prabowo's defence ministry has operationalized Modi's commitments. Defence procurement announcements from Jakarta carry long lead times, so absence of movement by mid-2025 would indicate the conversation remained symbolic rather than substantive. Second, track the implementation timeline for India-Indonesia DPI partnership pilots. The MEA will likely announce specific state-level or city-level deployment zones for UPI-style systems, probably in Java or Sumatra where digital adoption is highest. If announced by Q4 2024, success metrics (transaction volume, merchant adoption, rural penetration) become measurable by Q3 2025. Stalled announcements suggest Prabowo's team encountered bureaucratic or Chinese pressure friction.

Third, observe Indonesia's voting patterns and statements in ASEAN forums on South China Sea issues between now and mid-2025. If Prabowo's government increases maritime security posturing — whether through joint patrols with India, explicit support for UNCLOS interpretation, or upgraded port security measures — it validates that defence cooperation conversations generated real strategic alignment. Conversely, if Indonesia pivots toward accommodation on South China Sea disputes or deepens Chinese economic integration without parallel military diversification, it signals Modi's September meeting achieved rhetorical rather than structural buy-in. Watch especially for [[indonesia-china-defence-dialogue-2025|any simultaneous Chinese overtures to Jakarta on defence]], which would indicate Beijing recognizes this as a contested space and may offer countervailing incentives to Prabowo's team.
